section.title: The Wisdom of the Spirit
section.sectionRationale: Paul now shows the cross-shaped pattern in his own ministry and then explains how anyone comes to grasp the wisdom of the cross at all. He came not with eloquence but in weakness, resolved to know only Christ crucified, so that faith would rest on God's power. True wisdom is real, but it is the hidden wisdom of God, unknown to the rulers of this age who crucified the Lord of glory, and it is revealed only by the Spirit who searches the deep things of God. The natural person cannot receive it; but those who have the Spirit have the mind of Christ.
section.thesis: The wisdom of God centered in Christ crucified is hidden from human wisdom and from the rulers of this age, and can be received only through the Spirit who searches the depths of God; the natural man rejects it as folly, but those who have the Spirit are given the very mind of Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Paul's plain preaching in weakness, resting faith on God's power; the hidden wisdom of God ordained before the ages; the Spirit who alone reveals the deep things of God; and the natural man against the spiritual man who has the mind of Christ.

verses.text: When I came to you, brothers, I did not come with eloquence or wisdom as I proclaimed to you the testimony about God.
For I resolved to know nothing while I was with you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified.
I came to you in weakness and fear, and with much trembling.
My message and my preaching were not with persuasive words of wisdom, but with a demonstration of the Spirit’s power,
so that your faith would not rest on men’s wisdom, but on God’s power.

units.label: The hidden wisdom of God
units.note: ordained for our glory before the ages

verses.text: Among the mature, however, we speak a message of {{sophia|wisdom}}, but not the wisdom of this age or of the rulers of this age, who are coming to nothing.
No, we speak of the {{mysterion|mysterious}} and hidden wisdom of God, which He destined for our glory before time began.
None of the rulers of this age understood it. For if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.
Rather, as it is written: “No eye has seen, no ear has heard, no heart has imagined, what God has prepared for those who love Him.”

units.label: Revealed by the Spirit
units.note: the Spirit searches the deep things of God

verses.text: But God has revealed it to us by the {{pneuma|Spirit}}. The Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God.
For who among men knows the thoughts of man except his own spirit within him? So too, no one knows the thoughts of God except the Spirit of God.
We have not received the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God, that we may understand what God has freely given us.
And this is what we speak, not in words taught us by human wisdom, but in words taught by the Spirit, expressing spiritual truths in spiritual words.

units.label: The natural and the spiritual man
units.note: but we have the mind of Christ

verses.text: The {{psychikos|natural}} man does not accept the things that come from the Spirit of God. For they are foolishness to him, and he cannot understand them, because they are spiritually discerned.
The spiritual man judges all things, but he himself is not subject to anyone’s judgment.
“For who has known the mind of the Lord, so as to instruct Him?” But we have the {{nous|mind}} of Christ.

sophia.gloss: Wisdom, insight, skill. Paul insists that the gospel is not anti-intellectual: there is a true and profound wisdom in it. But it is not the wisdom of this age or its rulers, which is passing away; it is the hidden wisdom of God, centered in Christ crucified and known only by the Spirit.

mysterion.gloss: A mystery, a truth once hidden and now revealed by God. It is not a puzzle to be solved by cleverness but a secret purpose that God alone can disclose. The wisdom of the cross was ordained before the ages and hidden from the rulers of this age, and is now made known through the Spirit to those who love God.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind; here the Holy Spirit of God. The Spirit alone searches and knows the deep things of God, just as only a person's own spirit knows his thoughts. God's wisdom is therefore not reached by human insight but revealed by the Spirit whom believers have received, so that they can understand what God has freely given.

psychikos.gloss: Natural, unspiritual, belonging to the merely human soul apart from the Spirit. The natural person, however intelligent, cannot receive the things of the Spirit; they seem foolish and remain closed to him because they are discerned only by the Spirit. The word marks the great divide Paul draws between those with the Spirit and those without.

nous.gloss: Mind, understanding, the faculty of thought and perception. Paul's climactic claim is that believers have the mind of Christ. Through the indwelling Spirit, the people once unable to know God's thoughts now share the very understanding of their Lord, which no natural wisdom could ever attain.

basic.context: Paul reminds the Corinthians how he first came to them: not with impressive speech or worldly wisdom, but resolved to preach only Christ crucified, and in weakness and trembling, so that their faith would rest on God's power rather than on his eloquence. Then he explains that the gospel really is wisdom, but a hidden wisdom that the world and its rulers missed, so that they crucified the Lord of glory. This wisdom is known only because God reveals it by His Spirit, who alone knows the depths of God. The natural person cannot grasp it, but those who have the Spirit have the mind of Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul weaves his personal example together with teaching about revelation. He argues that the wisdom of the cross is real but must be given by the Spirit, and he supports this with Scripture, Isaiah on what God has prepared and on the mind of the Lord. The enduring point is that saving understanding is a gift of the Spirit, not an achievement of human intellect.
basic.originalLanguages: There is a true sophia, wisdom, but it is God's hidden wisdom, a mysterion, mystery, once concealed and now revealed. It is disclosed by the pneuma, the Spirit, who searches the depths of God. The psychikos, natural, person cannot receive it, but believers, given the Spirit, have the nous, the mind, of Christ.
basic.typology: The Spirit revealing the hidden wisdom of God fulfills the promise of God pouring out His Spirit on His people so that they know Him. The Lord of glory crucified by the rulers of this age fulfills the pattern of the rejected one whom God vindicates, as in the stone the builders rejected. What was hidden in the prophets is now unveiled in Christ by the Spirit.
basic.christ: Christ crucified remains the center: the one thing Paul resolved to know, and the Lord of glory whom the rulers failed to recognize and so crucified. The passage ends with the astonishing claim that believers have the mind of Christ, sharing His very understanding through the Spirit.
basic.god: God is the source of true wisdom, which He ordained before the ages for the glory of His people. He alone can reveal the depths of Himself, and He does so by His Spirit, who knows the thoughts of God as a person's spirit knows his own. Salvation and understanding alike are God's gift, freely given.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah speaks of what God has prepared for those who love Him. Jesus thanks the Father for hiding these things from the wise and revealing them to little ones. John promises the Spirit who guides into all truth. Romans cites the same question, who has known the mind of the Lord.
basic.oneStory: God's saving plan, centered in the cross, was hidden through the ages and is now revealed by the Spirit to those who love Him. What no human wisdom could discover, God freely gives, so that His people share the mind of Christ. The story moves from a wisdom hidden in God to a people indwelt by the Spirit and united to the Lord of glory.

context.literary: This chapter completes the argument begun at 1:17. Verses 1 to 5 give Paul's personal example, matching the theme of cross over eloquence; verses 6 to 16 explain the true wisdom and how it is received. The section pivots on the Spirit, moving from the hidden wisdom of God, to the Spirit who reveals it, to the two kinds of hearers, ending with the mind of Christ.
context.historical: Corinth admired traveling orators who dazzled with rhetoric and charged for their skill. Paul deliberately renounced that model, coming in weakness so the message, not the messenger, would carry the weight. The rulers of this age likely include both the political and spiritual powers behind the crucifixion, who failed to recognize the Lord of glory.
context.canonical: The passage joins the biblical theme of divine wisdom hidden and revealed, seen in Daniel, in Jesus' words about hidden things revealed to little ones, and in the promise of the Spirit of truth. It grounds the Christian conviction that Scripture and its saving truth are known by the Spirit's illumination, not by unaided reason.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model and defend a ministry that rests on the cross and the Spirit rather than human eloquence, and to teach that the wisdom of God is received only by the Spirit's revelation, so the Corinthians stop prizing worldly wisdom and rest their faith on God's power.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 1 to 5 describe Paul's practice, which carries a normative pattern for gospel ministry. Verses 6 to 16 teach abiding truth about revelation. The enduring principle is that faith and understanding rest on God's Spirit and power, not on human wisdom or persuasion.

debates.question: Who are the rulers of this age in verse 8?

views.label: Human authorities
views.case: The political and religious leaders, such as Pilate, Herod, and the Sanhedrin, who condemned Jesus without recognizing who He was, are those who crucified the Lord of glory.

views.label: Spiritual powers behind them
views.case: Paul often speaks of unseen rulers and authorities; on this view the demonic powers working through human agents are chiefly in view, defeated by the very cross they engineered.

debates.bearing: Whether human, spiritual, or both, the point holds: worldly power failed to grasp God's hidden wisdom and unwittingly served His saving plan at the cross.

debates.question: Does the natural man's inability in verse 14 mean people are entirely passive in coming to faith?

views.label: A real inability apart from the Spirit
views.case: Paul says the natural person cannot understand spiritual things; saving understanding requires the Spirit's work, underscoring that faith is God's gift and not a product of human wisdom.

views.label: An inability that does not remove responsibility
views.case: The natural person will not receive these things and is accountable for rejecting them; the Spirit's illumination and human response go together rather than cancelling one another.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading affirms that saving understanding is impossible without the Spirit, while holding that people remain responsible for their response to the gospel.

study.originalLanguages: Paul affirms a true sophia, wisdom, but not the wisdom of this passing age; it is the hidden wisdom of God, a mysterion, a secret purpose ordained before the ages and now unveiled. This wisdom is not attained but revealed, disclosed by the pneuma, the Spirit, who searches the very depths of God as a person's own spirit knows his thoughts. The divide is stark: the psychikos, the natural person living by the mere soul apart from the Spirit, cannot receive these things and calls them folly, while those given the Spirit are led to the climax of the chapter, that believers have the nous, the mind, of Christ.

typology.type: The Spirit revealing the wisdom of God
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the promise of God pouring out His Spirit so that His people know Him, now realized as the Spirit reveals the hidden wisdom of the cross.

typology.type: The Lord of glory rejected by the rulers
typology.fulfillment: Follows the pattern of the rejected one vindicated by God, the stone the builders rejected becoming the cornerstone, as the crucified Lord is exalted.

typology.type: What God has prepared, hidden then revealed
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's promise of unseen things prepared for those who love God is fulfilled as the Spirit makes known what no eye had seen.

study.christ: Christ crucified is both the content and the crown of this passage. Paul resolved to know nothing but Jesus Christ and Him crucified, staking his whole ministry on the message the world called folly. The rulers of this age, blind to God's wisdom, crucified the Lord of glory, a title that quietly confesses the deity of the one they killed. And the section rises to a breathtaking conclusion: through the Spirit, believers have the mind of Christ. The people who once could not know God's thoughts now share the understanding of their Lord Himself.
study.god: God is the fountain of a wisdom deeper than any human insight, a wisdom He ordained before time for the glory of His people and kept hidden until He chose to reveal it. Only God knows the depths of God, and He makes them known by His Spirit, who searches all things. Everything here is gift: the wisdom is freely destined, freely revealed, freely given. The God who cannot be discovered by human cleverness delights to disclose Himself by His Spirit to those who love Him.

crossReferences.note: The source of verse 9: no eye has seen what God has prepared for those who wait for and love Him, now revealed by the Spirit.
Jesus thanks the Father for hiding these things from the wise and revealing them to little ones, matching Paul's theme of hidden and revealed wisdom.
The Spirit of truth guides believers into all truth and takes what is Christ's to declare it, paralleling the Spirit's revealing work here.
Cited in verse 16, who has known the mind of the Lord; Paul answers that believers, by the Spirit, have the mind of Christ.

study.oneStory: God's saving wisdom, centered in the cross, was hidden through the ages and prepared before time for those who love Him. What no human wisdom could find, God has now revealed by His Spirit, so that His people understand what He has freely given and even share the mind of Christ. The whole story moves from a wisdom concealed in God to a Spirit-indwelt people joined to the crucified and glorified Lord.

quiz.q: Why did Paul come to Corinth in weakness, resolved to know only Christ crucified?

options.text: So that their faith would rest on God's power, not on human wisdom.
options.note: Verse 5 gives the reason: faith grounded in God's power rather than eloquence.

options.text: Because he had nothing else worth saying to them.
options.note: He chose this focus deliberately; the cross is the heart of the message, not a last resort.

options.text: Because he was ashamed of the gospel.
options.note: He was resolved to preach it; the weakness was to magnify the message, not to hide it.

options.text: Because eloquence and wisdom are always sinful.
options.note: The issue is misplaced trust in rhetoric, not that speech or thought is evil.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by the mysterious and hidden wisdom of God?

options.text: God's saving plan in the cross, ordained before the ages and now revealed by the Spirit.
options.note: Verses 7 and 10 describe a wisdom hidden then disclosed by God's Spirit.

options.text: A secret code only the most intelligent believers can decipher.
options.note: It is revealed to those who love God, not earned by intellect; a mystery is disclosed, not cracked.

options.text: A teaching so obscure that no one can really know it.
options.note: It is genuinely revealed by the Spirit, not left unknowable.

options.text: The accumulated wisdom of the philosophers of this age.
options.note: Paul contrasts it with the wisdom of this age, which is passing away.

quiz.q: How is the wisdom of God made known to believers?

options.text: God reveals it by His Spirit, who searches the deep things of God.
options.note: Verses 10 to 12 ground all understanding in the Spirit's revelation.

options.text: By careful study and superior human reasoning.
options.note: Study has its place, but Paul says the Spirit alone reveals the depths of God.

options.text: Through the teaching of the rulers of this age.
options.note: The rulers failed to grasp it and crucified the Lord of glory.

options.text: It cannot be made known to anyone at all.
options.note: God has revealed it to us by the Spirit.

quiz.q: Why can the natural man not accept the things of the Spirit?

options.text: Because they are spiritually discerned, and he lacks the Spirit who alone reveals them.
options.note: Verse 14 says such things seem folly and cannot be understood without the Spirit.

options.text: Because he is simply not clever enough to follow the argument.
options.note: The barrier is spiritual, not intellectual; even the brilliant cannot grasp them apart from the Spirit.

options.text: Because God has hidden them from everyone permanently.
options.note: They are revealed by the Spirit to those who love God, not hidden from all.

options.text: Because the things of the Spirit are actually foolish.
options.note: They only seem foolish to him; in truth they are the wisdom of God.

quiz.q: What astonishing claim does Paul make about believers in verse 16?

options.text: That through the Spirit they have the mind of Christ.
options.note: Believers share the very understanding of their Lord by the Spirit.

options.text: That they can fully comprehend God without any limits.
options.note: They have the mind of Christ, but this is a gift of the Spirit, not exhaustive knowledge of God.

options.text: That they no longer need Scripture or teaching.
options.note: The Spirit works through the word Paul speaks; it does not bypass revelation.

options.text: That they have earned wisdom by their own effort.
options.note: It is freely given by the Spirit, not achieved by human wisdom.

questions: Paul refused to lean on eloquence so that faith would rest on God's power. Where might your confidence be resting on human persuasion or personality rather than on God?
The rulers of this age missed God's wisdom and crucified the Lord of glory. How does this warn you against measuring truth by worldly power and prestige?
The wisdom of God is received only by the Spirit. How does this shape the way you read Scripture and pray before you try to understand it?
The natural person finds the things of God foolish. How can this deepen your patience and prayer for those who do not yet see the beauty of the gospel?
Believers are given the mind of Christ. What would it look like to trust that gift and think God's thoughts after Him in a decision you are facing now?

translationNotes.note: The Lord of glory is a striking title; applied to the crucified Jesus, it quietly confesses His deity, making the sin of the rulers all the graver.
This adapts Isaiah 64:4 (with echoes of Isaiah 65:17), on what God has prepared for those who love Him; Paul cites it to show the wisdom now revealed was long promised.
Natural man renders psychikos, the person living by the mere soul apart from the Spirit; it does not mean unintelligent, but unspiritual, lacking the Spirit needed to discern God's things.
The quotation is from Isaiah 40:13, who has known the mind of the Lord; Paul turns the rhetorical question into a claim that believers, by the Spirit, share the mind of Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 2:1-16. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule; wording is otherwise unchanged.
